our_argument_summary: SEC-3.5.5 argues that the dome site's '4 Refined' predictions are actually hidden falsifications. It makes three claims: (1) the site 'never identifies which four' predictions were refined; (2) 'Refined' as a category allows failed predictions to be reclassified without counting against the model — goalpost-moving in scientific terms; (3) 'the headline claims 0 falsified while the tracking page reports 4 falsified,' suggesting deceptive accounting. The section concludes that lack of transparency about which predictions were refined prevents independent assessment of whether refinements were legitimate or cosmetic.

holes_found.description: FACTUAL ERROR: The section states the dome site 'never identifies which four' predictions were refined. This is FALSE for V51.0. The V51.0 predictions page (monitor/baseline/03-predictions.txt) explicitly lists all four under 'Refined/Falsified:' — W019 (NMP weekly drift), W020 (Roaring 40s AAO threshold), W024 (Polaris elevation — explicitly labeled 'FALSIFIED 2026-03-15'), and W027 (Schumann elevation — 'refined to damping'). The V51.0 wins page also notes W024 and W027 individually. This is the exact kind of factual error that lets a dome defender discredit our entire section: 'The reviewer claims we don't identify them, but they're listed right on our predictions page. Did they even read it?'
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the opening sentence. Replace 'The page reports "4 Refined" predictions but never identifies which four' with something like: 'The V51.0 homepage reports "4 Refined (test windows)" without identifying them. The predictions page does list them: W019 (NMP drift), W020 (Roaring 40s), W024 (Polaris elevation), and W027 (Schumann). Examining each reveals a pattern: three are relabeled as "refined" with vague rationales, while one (W024) is explicitly marked "FALSIFIED" — suggesting the site can distinguish falsification from refinement but chooses not to for three of the four.' This is factually accurate AND makes a stronger argument.

holes_found.description: VERSION CONFUSION: The section says 'the headline claims "0 falsified" while the tracking page reports "4 falsified."' This was true for V50.6 (which said '39 Confirmed, 0 Falsified') but is NOT true for V51.0. The V51.0 homepage says '67 Confirmed Predictions | 9 Prospective | 95.2% Accuracy | 4 Refined (test windows)' — it acknowledged the 4 for the first time. And the AI context page (V50.10, lagging behind) says '54 Confirmed wins | 4 Falsified | 22 Pending.' So in V51.0, the site doesn't claim '0 falsified' — it uses 'Refined.' Our review explicitly targets V51.0, so this reference to '0 falsified' is stale and inaccurate. A dome defender would say: 'We updated our site to acknowledge these — the reviewer is citing our old version.'
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the '0 falsified' reference or explicitly frame it as a version comparison: 'In V50.6 (March 2026), the headline reported "0 Falsified." In V51.0, this became "4 Refined (test windows)" — an acknowledgment of the failures, but with language designed to minimize their significance. Meanwhile, the AI context page (still at V50.10) uses the word "Falsified" for the same four predictions. The site cannot agree with itself on whether these are falsifications or refinements.'

holes_found.description: MISSED OPPORTUNITY — Internal inconsistency within dome site. The dome site uses THREE different labels for the same four predictions: (1) Homepage: '4 Refined (test windows)'; (2) AI Context page (V50.10): '4 Falsified'; (3) Predictions page: 'Refined/Falsified' header with W024 explicitly marked 'FALSIFIED.' This internal inconsistency is devastating evidence that the dome model treats 'refined' and 'falsified' as interchangeable when convenient. Our section doesn't exploit this at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph: 'The dome's own site cannot agree on the terminology. The homepage says "4 Refined," the AI Context page says "4 Falsified," and the predictions page uses "Refined/Falsified" as a combined header — with W024 explicitly labeled "FALSIFIED." This inconsistency reveals that the author (or the AI system maintaining the site) recognizes these are falsifications but prefers softer language in the most-visible location (the homepage headline).'

holes_found.description: MISSED OPPORTUNITY — Individual analysis of each refined prediction. The section treats the four as a monolithic group when each tells a different story. W019 (NMP drift) and W020 (Roaring 40s) were weekly predictions with one-week test windows — they failed within their stated window and were relabeled as 'long-term unaffected.' W024 (Polaris) was a genuine falsification the site acknowledges. W027 (Schumann) had its entire mechanism inverted ('elevation' became 'suppression'). These are four different flavors of failure, and examining each individually is far more persuasive than a generic 'goalposts were moved' argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Expand with a brief analysis of each: 'W019/W020: Weekly predictions that failed within their one-week test window, then relabeled as "long-term unaffected" — but the original prediction was about the weekly timescale. W024: Acknowledged as falsified (a V12 formula bug, fixed in V13 — meaning the underlying model equation was wrong). W027: The predicted mechanism was inverted from elevation to suppression — not a refinement but a contradiction of the original prediction. Only W024 is honestly labeled; the other three use "refined" to avoid the word "falsified."'

holes_found.description: MISSING FROM DOCX: Section 3.5.5 exists in generate-html.js (HTML site) but does NOT appear in build-doc-v4.js (Word document). The DOCX version only has Section 3.5 ('Statistical Claims: The 9.2-Sigma Problem') as a single section, with none of the 3.5.1–3.5.8 subsections. Readers of the Word/PDF document never see this analysis of the refined predictions. This is a structural gap — the DOCX version of Part 3 is significantly weaker than the HTML version.
holes_found.recommendation: Add subsections 3.5.1 through 3.5.8 (or at minimum a summarized version) to build-doc-v4.js. At minimum, SEC-3.5.5 and SEC-3.5.6 (95.2% accuracy) are critical arguments that should appear in both output formats. Flag this as an open issue for the decider.

holes_found.description: The phrase 'hidden falsifications' in the section title is slightly overstated given V51.0's explicit acknowledgment. In V50.6 they were hidden (0 falsified reported while failures existed). In V51.0 they are acknowledged but relabeled. 'Relabeled Falsifications' or 'Disguised Falsifications' would be more accurate for the V51.0 era.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider retitling to '3.5.5 The "4 Refined" Predictions — Relabeled Falsifications' or adding a subtitle clarifying the version-aware framing.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest argument is the one about W024 (Polaris elevation). This prediction was falsified because of a formula bug in V12 that was fixed in V13. This means the dome model's core mathematical framework contained an error — not just a 'short test window.' If the formula itself was wrong, the 'prediction' was never valid. This is a much more damaging point than 'goalposts were moved.' It means the dome's equations are not stable — they change when predictions fail, which is the definition of post-hoc adjustment.
sources: monitor/baseline/02-wins.txt — W024 Polaris Elevation explicitly falsified 2026-03-15

stronger_arguments.description: W027 (Schumann elevation → suppression) is a mechanism inversion, not a refinement. The original prediction said the Schumann resonance would INCREASE during a specific condition. The 'refined' version says it would DECREASE (suppression). In any other scientific context, predicting the opposite direction of an effect is a falsification followed by a new hypothesis. Framing this as a 'refinement' is like saying 'I predicted it would go up, it went down, so I refined my prediction to say it goes down.' This is the clearest example of goalpost-moving among the four.
sources: monitor/baseline/03-predictions.txt — W027 'refined to damping' (suppression instead of elevation)

stronger_arguments.description: The pattern of version-dependent terminology reveals a deliberate communication strategy. V50.6: '0 Falsified' (hide failures). V50.10 AI Context: '4 Falsified' (acknowledge to AIs analyzing the site). V51.0 Homepage: '4 Refined (test windows)' (minimize for human readers). This progression suggests the author recognized the failures had to be acknowledged (perhaps after our review and AI analysis began) but chose the softest possible language for the most prominent placement. The fact that the AI context page uses 'Falsified' while the homepage uses 'Refined' for the SAME four predictions is evidence of audience-targeted framing.

kernel_of_truth.description: The concept of 'refining' a prediction is legitimate in science. When a test window is genuinely too narrow (e.g., a weekly geomagnetic prediction during an unusually quiet week), extending the test window IS a valid methodological improvement. Scientists routinely adjust experimental protocols after pilot studies. The dome model's willingness to acknowledge W024 as genuinely falsified (and to fix the formula bug) shows some degree of intellectual honesty.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The problem is not refinement per se — it's the asymmetry. All successes are locked in as 'Confirmed WINs' permanently, while failures are reclassified as 'Refined' and removed from the failure count. A legitimate research program would treat refinements symmetrically: if the test window was too short for W019 (NMP drift), was it also too short for some of the weekly predictions that happened to succeed (W009, W010, W011)? None of those were retroactively questioned. The one-way ratchet (successes stick, failures get renamed) is the methodological problem, and the dome's single honest falsification (W024) actually makes the relabeling of the other three more suspicious, not less.

citation_check.notes: Section contains no external citations — it references only dome site content. The dome site content references were checked against V51.0 baseline extracts and the 'never identifies which four' claim is factually wrong for V51.0.

deeper_analysis: SEC-3.5.5 is the weakest of the 3.5.x subsections because it contains a factual error that undermines its credibility. The irony is that the underlying argument is STRONG — the dome model does use terminology strategically, does reclassify failures, and does maintain an asymmetric accounting system. But the section leads with a false claim ('never identifies which four') that would let a defender dismiss the entire argument without engaging the substance. A rewrite that acknowledges the V51.0 site DOES list the four, then systematically examines each one, would be far more devastating. The version comparison angle (V50.6 said '0 falsified' → V51.0 says '4 Refined' → AI context says '4 Falsified') is an unexploited goldmine that shows the terminology evolving in response to scrutiny. The section should also cross-reference SEC-3.5.6 (95.2% accuracy) to show how the 'Refined' relabeling feeds into the accuracy arithmetic.
